title: Sundar Pichai on A.I. Backlash, the Future of Work and Google’s Next Era

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Sundar Pichai, welcome back to Hard Fork.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Thanks for having me, great to be here.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So the last time we had you on the show was in 2023, Bard, RIP, had just come out. Um, and I think at the time, the perception was that Google was catching up in AI. Um, how are you feeling about your position in the race these days?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Well that brings back memories, so it feels like eons ago, you know, those three years feel like a long time ago now. But I think it's staggering to see how much uh, both the technology is making progress, we've made progress as a company, um, and I think it's a very dynamic moment in moment in the industry. I think our models are at the frontier in some areas, uh, you know, and there are areas where we are behind the frontier, and so it's a combination. I think if you look at, uh, you know, overall capabilities including text, multimodality, voice, or audio, uh, reasoning in general, overall intelligence, I think we are very capable. Um, when it comes to agentic coding with tool use, and our instruction following, long horizon tasks, uh, I think, uh, we are a bit behind at this moment. But we are hard at work, uh, and, and the space is so dynamic, you know, all of the leading labs have their own pre-training cycles, so you have these cadences and they may not exactly match up. I think the moment is intense enough that if you're slightly off, uh, you know, three months ago people were like, we're ahead and no one could catch up with us, and then, you know, now the conversation flips. But that's part of the territory of being at the frontier. I think we are the only large company which is actually at that frontier, right? So one way to think about it is there are, you know, in this moment, uh, there are a couple of startups which have made extraordinary progress, and, and you know, we, uh, we've been deeply working on this for a long time. I think we took a big step forward with 1.5 Flash. It does address some of the areas we've been behind, um, and I think obviously getting it out in the real world and iterating with that data coming back, uh, is going to really help us. I think coding was the area where getting access to the data flows was important. I think we maybe quite didn't have the surface, uh, like Claude code as an example, or what Anthropic maybe had with Cursor too. And so getting activity with 1.5 Pro, we've been using it internally at Google for a while. I shared the token usage at Google I/O

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I've never seen anything like it internally. Right, we are doubling every week and people are really putting the models to work. And so that is helping us hill climb quite a bit. But, you know, the frontier is very dynamic, but I'm very, very optimistic and confident we'll push through there.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: It sounds like if there's like any place where you feel maybe not quite at the very lead where you actually want to be, it is coding. Is that right? Like is that where you're sort of putting the pressure?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Look, I think coding ends up being very foundational in everything, everything we do. So I think it's an important frontier to be on. There are areas in coding where we've been very good, you know, we've been very, very good at creating single-shot web frontends, everything. But in terms of these long-running, you know, tasks where serious developers are working on complicated codebases, I think we are making progress. It is just that there is a gap to the frontier where others are, but we are working, you know, we're well aware of it and, you know, making progress.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: 3.5 Flash has been out for a day. I do think it typically takes a few days to really put these models through their paces. We have seen some complaints though about pricing, model quality. Curious like what you've made of the reception so far.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Oh, you know, it's... I'm looking forward to being done with the, with my interviews and so on so that I can spend more time on, with the teams.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Wrap it up, guys.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Uh, no. Look, we, you know, I'm going to meet the teams right after this. I think we are definitely, you know, it'll take a day or two to settle in. I think it's a new model, in a new area where we've made some progress. There could be some regressions, but we will be able to quickly address them through, through our post-training, pretty quickly I think. There are some artifacts and behaviors we are seeing, which I think are easy to address, so we will. I do think given it was a day after us putting out a lot of things, I think we had tightened usage limits to avoid outages, but you will see us make progress on usage limits very soon. That is rightfully a source of frustration when you, when you encounter. I feel the same, but, but those are areas we will address pretty soon and make progress.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: It seems like one thing that some of the AI companies are succeeding at is focus. Just, you know, Anthropic and OpenAI have this sort of relentless focus on coding. OpenAI was criticized last year for sort of spreading their bets too thin, doing, trying to do too many things all at once. They've now sort of tightened that. Do you feel like Google is appropriately focused on coding? Or are all the other bets you're making taking away resources and time and focus from the main push?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: I think all of us saw sometime around... you know, there was an inflection point in coding, and, and I think we're all...

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Responding to it, uh, I, I, I think we all have, uh, you know, pretty serious strides around this area. And so I don't see it as an issue for focus. We are a large company and we have scale, so we, we will be able to focus on a few multiple things at the same time. I don't see it as any fundamental issues as much as we are making progress. We're going to make progress. I think we are in a moment in time in this field where 30 to 60 days look like five years. That's all it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Another thing that got a lot of attention this week was the changes that you all made to the search bar on the sort of front door of Google, the biggest change in 25 years. Uh, I think a lot of people expect that at some point the kind of normal Google sort of classic search interface goes away, the ten blue links maybe go away, and you just kind of have this AI mode as the default. Uh, but you haven't sort of done that yet. There's a lot of integration, but it's still—you still can get the ten blue links if you want them. Do you think that goes away at any point? That you sort of rip the Band-Aid off and just go full AI mode?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: You know, I think it's important to bring users along the journey as well as making sure the product is working for their expectations. So, you know, I try not to get—get ahead of that. I think it is very clear as we have evolved through these changes, people are responding positively. We can see it in the long-term metrics of the product in such a clear way, right? And, and so I think we understand that. Uh, but, you know, people want search to be fast. They—I do think through search people are looking to connect with what's out there on the web. So that's important to us. It's—it's all of that. Uh, so I think you're seeing us evolve the product, and I think you will continue to see it be methodical. But, you know, we didn't have AI mode a year ago, uh, but now a lot of people are experiencing it. I think we've made it more seamless to go there than before. And so it's a continuum. Uh, but I do see—sources and links will always be there as part of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: He was telling me on the ride down that he feels like he basically has not done a traditional Google search in the past year, that he's sort of fully doing these AI searches. When you hear that, are you like, 'Cool, like this is the kind of user that I want right now,' or does it send you a little chill because, you know, the sort of traditional search ads business is a pretty good one for you?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Well, uh, you know, I think we will, if anything in the AI mode, you know, in an agentic—these things are going to do a lot more for you than what we were able to do for users ten years ago. I think the economic value is always a function of the total value you're giving users. I think all of us would say over time the value—

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: we're providing as this increases, there's more competition, there are more choices. Uh, so I feel comfortable between a combination of subscription and ads, that the right models will continue to be there. Uh, Adam Smith's rules don't change in this new world.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Right. Let's uh talk about public perception. Uh, The New York Times, CNN did a poll this week found that only about 16% of people say that AI is mostly good, about 35% say it's mostly bad. What do you make of the AI backlash that we're seeing right now? And how much leverage do you think Google has to change that perception?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: I mean, AI is uh, you know, I've always viewed it as the most profound technology humanity will ever work on. It's progressing at an extraordinary pace. I, you know, humans aren't evolved uh to process that much change. And there are, I think people rightfully so uh are anxious about, you know, what what is the future that this technology will bring. So to me, you know, I I understand it. I think it feels to me natural with like such a profound technology shift. We've had far simpler technology shifts where there has been anxiety around those shifts. This is of a scale unlike anything we've we've seen before. I think the, we as an industry have to do a lot more to continue driving and showing the benefits that's possible with the technology. So that is something in our control. We have more work to do to make sure when we are scaling up the infrastructure investments etc, you know, what are the things we can do to to make some of that work better. But I think people's concerns are a bit more fundamental uh around the shift uh than all of that. I think a natural part of this is people are anxious about their economic future in this world, and you know you have a lot of conversation where people saying uh, you know, jobs are going to radically change, some of it will go away etc. I happen to think, you know, the outlook is better than those some of those uh dire predictions. But you know, as a society if you're hearing it, it's natural, you know, I I would be surprised if people weren't more anxious about it. I do think it's important because the change is happening so fast you need people in democracy, you need citizens to be engaged, be aware that this is happening and, you know, and and make their uh preferences known, that's what causes action in society. So I think there's something healthy about this dialogue too, which is happening, and given the pace at which the technology's moving forward, it seems right to me uh both the concerns and the fact that we need to take it seriously.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You're giving the commencement speech at Stanford next month. I'm sure you've noticed or heard that the...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: A bunch of commencement speakers have been booed recently by college students who are worried about AI. Um, what are you planning to tell the graduates about AI and do you have your like boo strategy in place?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Anytime we have driven technology progress, I think it uh helps drive progress in the world and in some ways these graduates are actually both going to be a big part of that driving that progress and also dealing with the impact of that technology. So I think we have to be very mindful of that and you know I've always been an extraordinarily optimistic about the next generation. I think we all always have this view in the world, you know we're anxious and we we worry about the next generation but I think the next generation rises to the challenge and you know builds a better world and so I view it as no different from those moments and you know my goal would be to share you know my experiences and and share that with them and that's what I'm looking to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You just pretend they're saying Google. It's like close enough, you know?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I'd be curious to hear a little bit more about your case that like you think that sure jobs may change a lot but you know you entry-level graduate the economic future is still bright for you. Like like what is that case in your mind?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: You know at a basic level I do think we are uh you know there is a new level of capability all of us are going to have to be able to do things and and you know I wasn't there like when spreadsheets rolled out to people. Like you know I wouldn't know how you did financial analysis before that like you know how did people do it? Right? Like...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I'll say it, I didn't do it. I had no idea how to do it.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Okay. But you know spreadsheets changed that and so there's an aspect of this I think it just is going to change the starting point for many many people. Just even coding, I think if you fast forward the progress we are seeing there, so many more people are going to be able to code in the world. Right? And you know I've heard you two might be examples of that and like you know in that journey but I think you're just at the leading edge of what is going to happen more and more. So I think those are the new serendipitous ways this will all work out that we underestimate. I think people are going to be uh more productive, they will have more time for leisure, all of that will simultaneously be true. Uh there are so many areas where today people's work you know involves a lot uh you know doctors have uh you know high burnout rates and it's you know it's because they train and uh you know their...

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Willingness to spend time with patients, taking care of patients, but you know, most doctors would tell you if you actually watch their time, the percentage of time they spend with patients is less, right? So I think AI will actually help them do more of that, right? And I think those are examples of it. The radiologist analogy has been fascinating. It's been now a decade running. I look at myself and I say, well, I've gotten a lot more scans in my life than my dad ever did. And each of the scans have like 10x the amount of information than his scans had because they were constrained by printing film versus us being digital. And I think that number is going to be 10x in 10 years. So where is that projection going into? You are actually going to need AI to keep up, right, with that demand coming.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: So I think it's non-linear how the impact of all this will be. This is not I don't want to be I don't want in any way to minimize every technology shift brings disruption with it. And you know, I think, you know, there will be disruption and we as a society need to be super serious about it and engage. And so some of the conversations I think are rightful in, like, you know, thinking through that. But I do think there are many positive dimensions to it, which are maybe not being talked about as much. And and also there is a overly deterministic dire scenarios which I quite don't agree with as part of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Let's talk about agents. Uh, because I feel like agents actually tie sort of into this question of, well, what is going to make us more productive in the future and and how will it change our job? Um, later this summer, you're releasing Spark, which I seems sort of intended to be an agent for the regular person. And I'm so curious to know, like, could you walk us through something this agent is doing for you personally?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: I've used it a lot more in my professional context, more to the context because of it was mainly available in my corp account, right, as part of it. In that context, I've definitely used it as it's super easy to use it to prepare for any meeting. I wish I had brought the prompt slash the output for, like, I just as a test case used it for Hard Fork.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I'll say, if you email us, we would flash it on the screen.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well, yeah, we want that.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: We want that.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: We want to know how Gemini trashed us.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: I'm not sure I'll allow that to, no, I'm just kidding. Partially kidding.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You used to see Casey's browser history.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Partially kidding. But I have had it in my personal account more recently. So again, here's a simple task I did. I just asked it to just look ahead...

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: My meetings and color-code it in categories so that I can keep sense of how I'm spending my time. I think, you know, it's extraordinary to watch it. It came back with like, you know, suggestions of two color-coding schemes and I just had to choose one. And it's actually like sci-fi, it's just like, you know, changes the color in the calendar. Personal meetings, health-related meetings, you know, time I'm spending at work, etc. That's an example of a personal query I just did, just to see what's happening, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: And, uh, but with agents, I think you have to give people a sense of—I think about this as what allowed us to get someone to sit in the back seat of a self-driving car, right? We did it in steps. And I think there's an element of that where with agents, if something unexpected happens, I think, you know, people will back off from this and, you know, so part of it is earning their trust, and so giving them a sense of control, transparency, but more importantly from a security standpoint, these systems, you know, can be hacked and so we want to make sure we are not ahead of the frontier in a—in a wrong way.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Speaking of meetings and your calendar, we hear that you're headed to the White House for some kind of AI executive order signing. Uh, what should the government be doing right now to regulate AI? Do you like this idea of a kind of pre-release strategy where the government gets to sort of see models before they're released and sign off on them? Is that a good idea? Is that potentially dangerous if it gives them the ability to censor or jawbone companies into releasing different kinds of models? What's your take on that?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Look, we'll have to wait and see the details of the full executive order, but they've been very—they've engaged with the industry in a very robust way, and I think the approach really balances, you know, innovation and oversight. We'll have to wait for the details to come out. But you know, there are a few areas which are coming up, you know, we will need more cross-industry, cross-industry government coordination. So it makes sense to me. Cyber is a great example of that. I think we all have to work together. It makes full sense to me if you've found an exploit which could impact a governmental agency, you know, the government needs to be prepared for it. So—so there is validity, but of course doing it in a moment with this important technology where it's important as a country to be at the frontier too, not doing it in a way where you're overly slowing things down, and maybe that balance has to shift as we reach more advanced levels of technology. But I think—I think to me this seems like a prudent approach. In general, I've, you know, part of what we are doing with, you know, building SynthID, open-sourcing it, and then sharing it with others, and more importantly, building a consortium together...

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: I think it's an example of, in a different area, these things only work together if we can come together as an industry. So, so, you know, I'm glad they're approaching it in that way.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Another sort of safety-related question. All of the big labs are racing toward what you call recursive self-improvement, so building AI systems that improve themselves rapidly. Um, do you think that can be done safely? And do you feel like you have a line of sight to it right now?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: You know, these models are getting better at, in a coding and agentic workflows. And so at some point, you know, you know, we did, you know, we did, you can see in AlphaDev today, you know, in over 12 hours it can build a simple OS from scratch, right? And, you know, that is genuinely those are multiple thousands of hours for somebody to do, right? So you are seeing some of that in work today. We're all in our products in some version or the other have agents and subagents and the orchestration of those agents building things together. It's a continuum, right? And I think, I think we are all definitely making some progress. But in the way people describe RSI, I don't think we are there yet. And, you know, and I, you know, that would represent a next level of acceleration. And I think, you know, and I think would have a lot of implications. But we aren't quite there yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Is there like a plan for uh-oh? Um, like I mean, great news, Sundar, like we just hit RSI, like is there a sort of, do we, do we break glass or what happens then?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: It's a great example of like, look, I think all responsible labs, I think if you're approaching moments like that, you would be, you know, consulting with, you know, it shouldn't be an internal conversation at that point. I think it has to be a much broader conversation, conversation than that. And I think we all have to avoid race conditions at those stages of AGI.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Right now all the labs are racing to get more compute. There seems to be bottomless demand for compute. They're hoarding it wherever they can, striking deals, um, you know, building their own data centers. Google is still selling access to TPUs to rivals and other companies in the race. Why? Why aren't you just keeping that for yourselves and your own models?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: I, I think each is not a constraint on the other, right? So as long as we can make enough chips, it's not a constraint. So the right way to think about it is we have our... we have GDM and our first-party services, if you can think about that as a company, business, cash flows, you're planning for that. And then you've Google Cloud, which is a business.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: which has revenue cash flows and you're doing long term plan and you're planning for that, right? So if we didn't have cloud and we weren't providing, we wouldn't be planning those chips anyway. Right? And so that's at the simplest level. You obviously, you know, it's a bit more complex than that. But there're a lot of advantages of providing TPUs to others, the fact that researchers at Anthropic are using TPUs is what will allow us to make, in addition to us, allows us to make the best hardware in terms of next generation. And and by the way, we use Nvidia's chips too and their next generation chips are incredible and so we use that and we we work too. When you're running platforms, when you have a platform side of the business and you know, I I've always worked on many platforms in my life and be it Chrome or Android or Google Cloud, you know, why why would you ever open source something or why do you provide this technology? I mean, all that makes sense on its own merits. I do think there are advantages like I mentioned. It allows us to stay at the frontier, you know, economies of scale helps in in various ways. And so it makes a lot of sense that way.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah. The last time we had you on, we asked you about AGI and your feelings about the term and at the time you responded that it didn't really matter whether we've reached AGI or not because the systems are going to be very, very capable and Google's strategy should be the same. I noticed that you did not say AGI in your keynote. Demis did, but but you did not. What's your relationship with the term AGI today and sort of the idea that all of this progress is building towards something singular and world-changing?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Oh, we are, you know, there is inevitable progress towards AGI that's happening, I've long understood it and you know, otherwise I wouldn't have pivoted the company 10 years ago to like put that technology at the heart and center of the company. All I meant by that statement was even in the scenario AGI is going to take 10 years, the technology which three years out will be so much more powerful than what we have today that I don't want people to think because AGI is 10 years out, you don't need to act or prepare. That is all my statement means in those contexts.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Are you AGI-pilled?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Well I, I absolutely, you know, I'm sure that the technology is making foundational progress towards AGI. I'm less able to predict with certainty whether it's in the 3 to 5 year time frame or 5 to 10 year time frame. The rate of progress over the last 1 to 2 years has made me feel it's on the closer side than not. And you know, I just don't, you know, in your role running one of the largest companies in the world which has a responsibility to society, the language I choose to use around it might be different than than other people. But I think, you know, I think we are on that path.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: As a company in terms of, uh, you know, 10 years ago at the IO stage, I announced TPUs and AI-first data centers, uh, you know, yes, clearly understood where this technology is headed.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Maybe as a last question, one of the more memorable phrases I think from the keynote this year did come from Demis when he said that we're in the foothills of the singularity. Can you tell us like concretely what that means from Google's perspective and should people be excited about that or afraid or both?

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Look, I've had many conversations with Demis obviously on this topic and I think, I think in this context he's, I think he's defining singularity as the advent of AGI. I think he's talking in that context and, you know, I think if you think, regardless of, if I remember I think he had, he had kind of articulated by 2030 or so, I think if you believe that, it makes sense to you that that's what you're articulating and, you know, it's as simple as that. I think for him that's how he defines singularity and, and I think Demis, myself, many others, we all feel it's important to, if that's what you believe, it's important to articulate that because we're all at the frontier building this technology and, you know, hopefully people are listening and I think it's important to as a society we're internalizing that and, and getting ready for it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Sundar Pichai, thanks so much for coming.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Thank you, sir.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Thanks.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Good to talk to you.

full_transcript.speaker: Sundar Pichai
full_transcript.text: Appreciate it. Take care.

highlights: Pichai acknowledges that while Google is at the AI frontier, the company is currently trailing some competitors in agentic coding and handling complex, long-horizon developer tasks.

To address anxiety over job displacement, Pichai compares AI’s impact to the introduction of spreadsheets, arguing that the technology will raise the baseline for human productivity rather than simply eliminating roles.

Regarding the future of Google Search, Pichai describes a methodical "continuum" toward an AI-first interface, though he maintains that connecting users to web sources and links will remain a core part of the experience.

Pichai confirms that Google is on an "inevitable path toward AGI" and aligns with the perspective that society is currently in the "foothills of the singularity," necessitating proactive preparation for profound technological shifts.
summary:
In this interview on the *Hard Fork* podcast, Google CEO Sundar Pichai discusses the company’s current standing in the AI race, positioning Google as one of the few organizations operating at the "frontier" of the technology. While he acknowledges that Google has faced a gap in agentic coding and long-horizon tasks compared to competitors like Anthropic and OpenAI, he emphasizes the rapid iteration cycles fueled by the Gemini 1.5 models. Pichai also addresses the evolution of Google Search, describing a methodical transition toward AI-integrated experiences that aims to balance user expectations with the traditional web ecosystem of links and advertisements.

The conversation shifts toward the broader societal impact of AI, where Pichai acknowledges the public's anxiety regarding economic futures and the unprecedented pace of change. He offers an optimistic perspective, comparing AI to the advent of the spreadsheet, suggesting it will lower the barrier to complex skills like coding and allow professionals to focus on higher-value tasks. He concludes by confirming Google’s commitment to achieving Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), noting that while timelines remain uncertain, the industry is effectively in the "foothills of the singularity." This progression, he argues, requires a careful balance of innovation, government oversight, and proactive societal engagement.
description:
When Sundar Pichai, Google’s chief executive, took the stage at Google I/O 2026, he outlined a vision of an A.I.-assisted future aimed at making life just a little bit easier in myriad ways.

This comes, however, against the backdrop of a growing public opinion problem for A.I. companies: graduates have booed mentions of artificial intelligence at commencements around the country, and a recent New York Times/Siena poll found that 35 percent of respondents viewed the technology as “mostly bad,” compared with just 16 percent who viewed it as “mostly good.”

After the I/O conference, Kevin Roose and Casey Newton, the hosts of "Hard Fork," sat down with Pichai to talk about Google’s place in the A.I. race, whether the usefulness of the company’s products will be enough to overcome public skepticism and what he expects for the future.

0:01 Google’s position in the AI race
3:19 Is Google behind on coding? 
4:02 Reactions to Gemini 3.5 Flash performance and pricing
5:30 What is Google’s primary focus? 
6:37 Is AI Mode replacing Google Search? 
9:16 Sundar Pichai on A.I. Backlash
11:55 Commencement speech 
13:10 A.I.’s impact on the labor market and jobs for graduates
16:40 How does Sundar use Gemini Spark? 
19:22 Should the Government regulate A.I.? 
21:14 A.I. Safety and RSI (Recursive Self improvement) 
23:11 Google’s compute strategy
25:13 How do you define AGI? 
27:13 “We’re in the foothills of singularity”

topics: Google's competitive position in the AI race
The integration of AI into Google Search and its business impact
Public skepticism regarding AI and its impact on the future of work
AI safety, government regulation, and the path to AGI
The development and practical utility of AI agents
